coredata.dc:title: Multi-functional polysaccharides composite film containing cashew and jik leaf carbon dots: Properties and bioactivities

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) from the leaf powders of cashew (C-CDs), jik (J-CDs) and the mixed cashew/jik (1:1) (CJ-CDs) using hydrothermal method were synthesized and characterized. All three CDs were incorporated into chitosan/starch composite (CS) film at the level of 3 % (w/w). Microstructure images revealed that all films had slight differences in surface and internal structure. Thickness of the CS film increased with the inclusion of fillers (CDs). Inclusion of CDs slightly reduced the tensile and water vapor barrier properties of the CS film. CS film containing C-CDs exhibited the improved elasticity, UV barrier and antioxidant activity among all the films tested. Color and opaqueness of the resulting films were governed by the yellowish CDs. Distinctive peaks of resulting CS film were detected in FTIR spectra, while all CDs had no marked effect on the spectra. The release of CDs from films was more pronounced in water than in alcoholic solutions (10–95 % ethanol). Strongest antibacterial activities against Listeria monocytogenes and Escherichia coli were recorded for CDs added film, in which the highest activities were found for CS/CJ-CDs film. Thermal stability was also enhanced in CS/CJ-CDs film. Thus, the CS film loaded with CDs, especially from the mixture of cashew and jik leaf powders could serve as a promising active packaging for inactivation of some pathogens in food products.

head.ce:title: Multi-functional polysaccharides composite film containing cashew and jik leaf carbon dots: Properties and bioactivities

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) from the leaf powders of cashew (C-CDs), jik (J-CDs) and the mixed cashew/jik (1:1) (CJ-CDs) using hydrothermal method were synthesized and characterized. All three CDs were incorporated into chitosan/starch composite (CS) film at the level of 3 % (w/w). Microstructure images revealed that all films had slight differences in surface and internal structure. Thickness of the CS film increased with the inclusion of fillers (CDs). Inclusion of CDs slightly reduced the tensile and water vapor barrier properties of the CS film. CS film containing C-CDs exhibited the improved elasticity, UV barrier and antioxidant activity among all the films tested. Color and opaqueness of the resulting films were governed by the yellowish CDs. Distinctive peaks of resulting CS film were detected in FTIR spectra, while all CDs had no marked effect on the spectra. The release of CDs from films was more pronounced in water than in alcoholic solutions (10–95 % ethanol). Strongest antibacterial activities against Listeria monocytogenes and Escherichia coli were recorded for CDs added film, in which the highest activities were found for CS/CJ-CDs film. Thermal stability was also enhanced in CS/CJ-CDs film. Thus, the CS film loaded with CDs, especially from the mixture of cashew and jik leaf powders could serve as a promising active packaging for inactivation of some pathogens in food products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Plastic pollution is widespread across the globe, causing significant harm to land and water sources. Despite their negative impact on the environment and human health, synthetic plastics remain the top choice for food packaging because of their cost-effectiveness and ability to withstand pressure and temperature for producing bags, bottles and other containers. Currently, both industry and academia have focused on more sustainable materials, specifically biodegradable polymers, to tackle plastic pollution in environment (Roy et al., 2024). Hence, there is an increasing interest in creating environmentally friendly, non-toxic and biodegradable food packaging with natural polymers in the food sector. Polysaccharides, e.g., starch and chitosan, are commonly employed in producing biodegradable packaging owing to their benefits such as being edible, environmentally friendly, renewable, cost-effective and having good film-forming properties (Estevez-Areco, Guz, Famá, Candal, & Goyanes, 2019). However, these green packaging materials have limitations for preserving foods, mainly because of their poor barrier properties with the lack of bioactivities (Fan et al., 2023).

Starch is a widely used edible and biodegradable polysaccharide, and it is easily soluble in water and composed of α-D-glucosides (Bangar, Whiteside, Ashogbon, & Kumar, 2021). It is plentiful, affordable, biocompatible and known for its great film-forming abilities and has low oxygen permeability (Jiménez, Fabra, Talens, & Chiralt, 2012). Nonetheless, their usage as packaging films is restricted due to certain disadvantages, including hydrophilic character and poor mechanical properties (Ruggero, Carretti, Gori, Lotti, & Lubello, 2020). Starch was blended with other biopolymers such as chitosan or gelatin to enhance the properties of resulting film. Chitosan is also the most common natural biopolymer and produced by converting chitin from crustacean shells or molds and yeast cell walls through deacetylation (Shin & Kim, 2021). The active amino and hydroxyl groups of chitosan could form hydrogen bonds, which enhance the durability of the packaging film (Arnon, Granit, Porat, & Poverenov, 2015). Chitosan with the profound film forming ability is commonly utilized in food packaging because of its inexpensive nature, ability to break down naturally, compatibility with biological systems and safety (Estevez-Areco et al., 2019). To enhance their preservative properties of those films, antimicrobial agent and antioxidant have been incorporated (Fan et al., 2023), which can be considered as active packaging (Dutta & Sit, 2023).

Recently, carbon dots (CDs), a nano-filler, have gained an augmenting interest in food packaging since CDs have shown antimicrobial or antioxidant properties (Murugan et al., 2024). CDs possess a complicated chemical composition consisting of a carbon-based core and functional groups on its surface. The basic configuration of CDs is determined by the synthesis method and the carbon source used as the raw material (Roy, Ezati, Rhim, & Molaei, 2022). However, surface functional groups typically originate from a carbon source. CDs possess surface functional groups (such as hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups), which exhibit exceptional properties like UV protection, luminescence, antibacterial and antioxidant activities. Their small size and adjustable surface functional groups contribute to strong interactions and high adsorption on biological surfaces. These CDs are prepared by natural or metal-based materials, in which natural based counterpart has gained more attention due to the low-toxicity and availability (Deepika, Kumar, & Gaikwad, 2023). Natural based CDs are mostly synthesized from agro and seafood processing wastes (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023a). Among all the synthesis processes of CDs, hydrothermal method is the most widely used. It is a low cost and easy, green process for the synthesis of CDs. The high temperature facilitates the dissolution of the carbon precursors in water. As the temperature rises, the organic components undergo pyrolysis, conversion to smaller organic molecules and carbonization. The formation of carbon skeletons through the dehydration of biomass results in smaller carbon-rich structures. Moreover, the surface charge, functional groups and size of CDs greatly enhance the antioxidant and antibacterial properties (Roy et al., 2022). Inclusion of CDs with polymer matrix greatly enhanced the barrier (UV light and water vapor) and bioactivities (Deepika et al., 2023; Murugan et al., 2024).

Anacardium occidentale L. (Cashew) and Barringtonia acutangula (Jik) are widely distributed in Thailand and southeast Asia (Chantaranothai, 1995; Kongkachuichai, Charoensiri, Yakoh, Kringkasemsee, & Insung, 2015). Leaves from both trees consisted of several bioactive phenolic compounds associated with antimicrobial and antioxidant activities (Ahmad, Sae-leaw, Zhang and Benjakul, 2024; Sinlapapanya et al., 2022). Moreover, plant phenolic compounds have multiple bioactivities, including cardioprotective, anti-inflammatory, anti-cancer and hypoallergenic actions (Chotphruethipong, Benjakul, & Kijroongrojana, 2017). Ethanolic cashew and jik leaf extracts exhibited metal chelation, DPPH and ABTS radical-scavenging efficacies and antibacterial activities (Ahmad, Sae-leaw, Zhang and Benjakul, 2024; Sinlapapanya et al., 2022). CDs from these leaves could be the potential source of natural active filler in food packaging. Therefore, this study aimed to prepare and characterize CDs from different leaf powders and to examine the properties and bioactivities of active chitosan/starch composite films incorporated with CDs from leaf powders.

Chitosan (MW: ⁓190–310 × 103 kDa; degree of deacetylation: ⁓75–85 %; viscosity: 200–800 cps) and all chemicals for antioxidant assays were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Corn starch was procured from Gel-Tec Co. Ltd. (Seoul, Korea). Glycerol was acquired from Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co. Ltd. (Siheung, Gyeonggi-do, Korea). Acetic acid was supplied by Samchun Chemical Co., Ltd. (Gangnam-gu, Seoul, Korea). Microbial media were obtained from HiMedia (Mumbai, India).

Leaves of cashew and jik were collected from trees in Hat Yai (Songkhla, Thailand). After cleaning with running water, the leaves were dried in a tray dryer (45 °C for 16 h) until the moisture content of approximately 4 % was attained. Dried leaves were blended and sieved using 80 mesh stainless-steel sieve. The obtained powder was termed as cashew leaf powder (CLP) and jik leaf powder (JLP). The powders were packed in zip-lock bag and then kept at 4 °C.

Hydrothermal method was adopted to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) from both CLP and JLP. CLP and JLP (2 %, w/v) were dispersed separately with distilled water in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor and heated using a muffle furnace at 200 °C for 6 h (Murugan et al., 2024). In addition, the mixture of CLP and JLP (1:1 ratio) was also used to prepare CDs following the same procedure. After cooling, the obtained yellowish brown mixtures were centrifuged and filtered using a membrane filter (pore size of 0.20 μm) and named as CDs. CDs from CLP, JLP and CLP/JLP mixture were named as C-CDs, J-CDs and CJ-CDs, respectively. All CDs were transferred into the falcon tube and stored at 4 °C before further analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and analyses of chitosan/starch films containing various CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan and starch film forming solutions (FFS) were prepared separately (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023b). Chitosan (2 %) and starch (2 %) were solubilized in acetic acid (1 %, v/v) and distilled water, respectively. Both FFS were mixed at 1:1 ratio and stirred for 1 h and named as chitosan/starch FFS (CS-FFS). Glycerol (30 %, based on polymer weight) was employed as a plasticizer. Thereafter, C-CDs, J-CDs and CJ-CDs were incorporated into CS-FFS at the level of 3 % (w/w) and stirred for another 1 h until complete dissolution was achieved. FFS (200 ml) were cast evenly on the Teflon film-coated glass plate (240 × 300 mm2) and air-dried for 48 h (23 ± 2 °C). Film samples were manually peeled off, followed by being equilibrated in an environmental chamber (50 ± 5 % RH at 25 ± 0.5 °C) for 48 h The developed films without and with CDs (C-CDs, J-CDs and CJ-CDs) were named as CS (control film), CS/C-CDs, CS/J-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films, respectively.

Photographs of all the films were captured using a smart phone camera. The surface (magnification: 3.0k ×) and cross-section (magnification: 1.3–1.4k ×) morphologies of all films were visualized at acceleration voltage of 10 kV using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Hitachi, SU3900, Tokyo, Japan) (Murugan et al., 2024).

Thickness of different films was evaluated with the aid of digital micrometer (Digimatic Micrometer, QuantuMike IP 65, Mitutoyo, Japan). Films were subjected to tensile testing using an Instron Universal Testing Machine (Model 5565, Canton, MA, USA) as per the ASTM D 882–88 standard protocol. Young's modulus (YM), tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EAB) of the films were evaluated and recorded (Khan et al., 2023a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA)
ce:section.ce:para:
WVP of all the films was tested and computed (Khan et al., 2023a). Briefly, films (70 × 70 mm2) were cut and tightly sealed to the gravimetric cup containing deionized water (18 mL) and then kept in an environmental chamber (Jeio Tech Co. Ltd., FX1077, Ansan, South Korea) at 50 % RH and 25 °C. The testing cups were weighed every 1 h for totally 9 h, and a plot between the weight change of the cup versus time was prepared, where the slope displayed the water vapor transmission rate (WVTR, g.m2.s) of the CS films. The WVP (gm/m2.Pa.s) values were computed as follows:
W V P =
(
W V T R ×
L
)
Δ
p
where L represents the average thickness (m) and Δp denotes the difference in partial water vapor pressure (Pa) across the film.

The surface hydrophobicity of all the films was examined with the contact angle analyzer (Surface Electro-Optics Co., Ltd., Phoneix 150, Kunpo, South Korea). Film sample (25 × 100 mm2) was fixed horizontally on the movable workbench (70 × 110 mm2, black Teflon-coated steel). Then a droplet (10 μL) of distilled water was transferred by a micro syringe on the film's surface (Riahi, Khan, Shin, Rhim, & Kim, 2024). Once the droplet became stabilized, the WCA was measured. The testing was analyzed ten times for each sample.

Color of films was measured with the aid of a colorimeter (Konica Minolta, CR-400, Tokyo, Japan) (Riahi et al., 2024). ΔE* (total color difference) was computed.

ΔE* was computed based on ΔL*, Δa* and Δb* when compared to the corresponding values of a white standard.

UV–Vis transmittance (200–800 nm) of film samples was evaluated using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Mecasys Optizen POP Series UV/Vis, Seoul, Korea). The UV barrier was performed (Murugan et al., 2024) and the percentage of blocking toward UV-A and UV-B was computed as follows:

where, dλ is the bandwidth of the film and T(λ) is the average transmittance of the film at the wavelength λ.

The opaqueness value was computed at 600 nm with UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Tagrida, Nilsuwan, Gulzar, Prodpran, & Benjakul, 2023).

where, T 600 represents fractional transmittance at 600 nm and X represents film thickness (mm).

FTIR spectra of all films were analyzed in the range of 500 to 4000 cm-1 using 32 scans and 4 cm–1 resolution with a Bruker FTIR spectrometer (Model IFS 66 V/S, Bruker Co., Ettlingen, Germany) (Riahi et al., 2024). Spectra was normalized and recorded.

The release of CDs from the CS films in varying food stimulants was tested (Khan et al., 2023a). Films (25 × 25 mm2) were placed in 20 mL of different solutions mimicking food simulants (water, 10, 50 and 95 % ethanol to represent aqueous and lipid-based food systems). The mixtures were shaken continuously for 180 min (25 °C) before filtration using a filter paper. The filtrate was read at 265 nm (C-CDs), 250 nm (J-CDs) and 275 nm (CJ-CDs) (based on UV absorption peak of different CDs) to monitor the release of CDs from CS/C-CDs, CS/J-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films, respectively with 30-min interval up to 180 min.

Small pieces of films (0.2 g) were mixed with 20 mL of 80 % methanol and stirred intermittently for 16 h using a magnetic stirrer and then centrifuged (8500 × g, 15 min) (Murugan et al., 2024). The obtained supernatants were tested for DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activities (DPPH-RS-A and ABTS-RS-A, respectively) and ferric reducing antioxidant power (FRA-P). Activities were expressed as μmol Gallic acid equivalent (GA)/10 g sample. Metal chelating activity (MC-A) was also determined and recorded as μmol EDTA equivalent (EE)/10 g sample.

The antimicrobial efficacies of all films against Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) were analyzed using total viable colony count (TVCC) protocol (Roy, Ezati, & Rhim, 2021). All the glassware and utensils used in the experiment were sterilized (121 °C, 30 min) and all film samples were UV irradiated for 15 min before the analyses. TVCC was monitored every 3 h for totally 12 h.

Thermal behaviors of films were determined using thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA-8000, Perkin Elmer, Norwalk, CT, USA). Films were placed in a standard aluminum pan and sealed tightly. A scanned heating rate of 10 °C/min was used under a nitrogen flow of 20 mL/min, in the range of 30–800 °C (Tagrida et al., 2023). Weight loss was also computed and reported as a function of temperature.
One-way ANOVA was conducted and means were compared using Duncan's Multiple Range Test. The analysis of data was done with the SPSS software (version 27.0 for Windows, SPSS Inc, Chicago, IL, USA).

Appearance of all films containing without and with CDs is depicted in Fig. 1 a. Control film was more transparent than those added with CDs. In general, CS film is clear and transparent (Khan et al., 2023b). Inclusion of CDs into CS films resulted in yellowish color, most likely due to the yellowish color of CDs. Notably, CS/J-CDs film showed lighter color and more transparent than other films (CS/C-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs), as witnessed by less opaqueness value (Table 2). Thus, the addition of CDs as the fillers into film matrix altered the transparency of resulting films to some extent.

Both surface and cross-sectional images of CS films were slightly affected by CDs incorporation, depending on the types of CDs added. All films had finer structure with no gaps, cracks or bubbles. Notably, no fibrous structure on the surface of CS film was found. Smoother surface was observed for both CS/J-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films. Moreover, the fibrous structure in cross-section of CS film was greatly enhanced in both CS/C-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films. CDs might be involved in the interaction of polymers, in which the larger bundles could be formed. On the other hand, the homogenous structure with very fine fibers was attained in the control and CS/J-CDs film. Microstructure is essential for determining properties of the films, in which the biopolymers interacted or entangled each other (Fig. 1b and c). Inclusion of hydrophilic CDs to biopolymeric films led to entanglement by which ordered structure could formed (Khan et al., 2023a). Generally, the addition of nanofillers greatly aids in the enhanced interaction between the polymers and nanofillers, which also resulted in improved mechanical and physical attributes (Fu, Sun, Huang, Li, & Hu, 2019). Cross-sectional images revealed that CS/C-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs film were thicker than others, as indicated by higher thickness (Table 1 ). Overall, C-CDs promoted the smooth and homogeneous surface, while cross-sectional structure became more fibrous with addition of J-CDs. This was plausibly due to the varying reactivities of compounds in different CDs toward the biopolymers (chitosan and starch).

Thickness of all films is tabulated in Table 1. CS film showed less thickness (62.30 µm) than other films (P < 0.05). The addition of active fillers generally increased the thickness of film. Thickness of CS/J-CDs film upsurged by 10.27 %, compared with that of the control film. The increases in thickness by 15 % were found for both CS/C-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films. Similar findings were also documented (Khan et al., 2023a; Murugan et al., 2024). Overall, higher thickness was recorded when all CDs were incorporated into the films, however no significant differences were observed between CDs added films (P > 0.05). Upsurge in the thickness might be caused by the augmented CDs or solid content in the films. Furthermore, the interaction and alignment between polymers might be impeded in the presence of CDs, bringing about less ordered matrix arrangements. This might lead to a relatively protruded network of resulting films. Thus, the addition of CDs directly affected the film thickness.

The film's mechanical characteristics have a major effect on its ability for commercialization, in which packaging films need to have the adequate strength and integrity to avoid any breakage or leakage when storing and distributing foods (Min, Ezati, & Rhim, 2022). YM, TS and EAB of resulting films are presented in the Table 1. Control films showed higher YM (2.20 GPa) than others (P < 0.05). Moreover, the inclusion of CDs decreased YM of the film. YM was decreased by 8.64, 30.45 and 13.64 % for CS/C-CDs, CS/J-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films in comparison with that of control film, respectively. Reduced YM might be attributed to decreased molecular interaction between CS molecules by CDs. Drop in YM could be due to the introduction of external particles that might increase the flexibility of films (Zaman, Lin, & Phing, 2018). This result was clearly reflected by the augmented EAB of resulting films (Table 1). Notably, C-CDs and CJ-CDs added film had higher YM than J-CDs added films (P < 0.05) and similar EAB was obtained between CS/J-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films (P > 0.05). Thus, the sources of CDs had the influence on tensile properties of the resulting films.

The control film showed the highest TS (90.62 MPa) (P < 0.05). TS is governed by intermolecular interaction between biopolymers. The addition of starch to the chitosan resulted in the enhanced TS of the films, which might be attributed to the formation of stronger inter-molecular hydrogen bonds between -OH of the starch and -NH3 + of the chitosan (Xu, Kim, Hanna, & Nag, 2005). However, the addition of CDs gradually reduced the TS of chitosan/starch films. Reduction of TS by 14.80, 25.88 and 22.82 % was observed for CS/C-CDs, CS/J-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films, respectively (P < 0.05). Lowered TS might be associated with the CDs' hydrophilic nature, which possibly resulted in increased moisture content of films (Oladzadabbasabadi et al., 2023). Similar finding was reported for CDs added films (Murugan et al., 2024). Lowered TS was caused by the inclusion of foreign particles, which plausibly weaken the integrity of the film matrix (Li, Yin, Yang, Tang, & Wei, 2012). CS/C-CDs film exhibited greater TS among CDs added films (P < 0.05). Reduction in TS was generally in line with the decrease in YM and the rise in EAB.

Lowest EAB was found in the control film (28.43 %) (P < 0.05). Notable flexibility enhancement was observed in CDs added films including CS/C-CDs (37.76 %), CS/J-CDs (35.45 %) and CS/CJ-CDs (36.25 %) films (P < 0.05). When CDs were introduced into film matrix, the size and sources of each CDs greatly influenced the properties of resulting films. Upsurge in EAB was plausibly caused by the interaction between CDs and glycerol via hydrogen bond. This might lead to greater separation between molecules and allows for better movement of polymer segments (Oladzadabbasabadi et al., 2023). This result clearly indicated that most of the empty spaces were filled by the nano-sized CDs. Inclusion of CDs were evenly spread throughout the CS film, leading to the enhanced discontinuity of the polymer matrix and the increased flexibility and reduced rigidity. Enhanced EAB was observed for CS/CJ-CDs film, which was 2.26 % non-significantly higher than that of CS/J-CDs film (P > 0.05). Overall, elasticity of resulting films was enhanced by the inclusion of CDs, regardless of sources.

A package having a good barrier against water can prevent food deterioration from moisture and keep the intact texture (Oladzadabbasabadi et al., 2023). Control film showed the lowest WVP amongst all films (P < 0.05). Higher WVP was attained in CDs added films (Table 1). CDs had the hydrophilicity, thus providing the passage of water vapor through a film via various physical processes (Qu, Wei, & Sun, 2018). Addition of hydrophilic CDs to biopolymeric film resulted in the rising number of free hydroxyl groups (Liu et al., 2023). Water vapor barrier property of films decreased as the addition of CDs was carried out. WVP values were increased around 27, 43 and 24 % for CS/C-CDs, CS/J-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films, respectively. Nano-sized CDs were unable to occupy the empty spaces completely in the polymer chain and alter the winding path for the water molecules (Khan et al., 2023a). Simultaneously, the formation of hydrogen bonds with water vapor occurred. Moreover, C-CDs and CJ-CDs had higher ability to block the passage of water molecules than J-CDs as evidenced by the lower WVP of the formers. Therefore, WVP of CDs added films was determined by source of CDs, in which those CDs with different sizes, morphology and hydrophilicity, etc. had the impact on the arrangement of film matrix in different fashions, resulting in the varying WVP.

The contact angle of a liquid drop on film's surface indicates if the surface is hydrophobic or hydrophilic. The angle can range from 0 to 180°. The value less than 20° indicates a hydrophilic surface that interacts well with water, whereas the value over 70° suggests a hydrophobic surface (Hernández, Ludueña and Flores, 2024). WCA of control film was found to be 81.42° (Table 1). However, the WCA values of all CDs added films declined. WCA of CS/C-CDs (74.68°), CS/J-CDs (71.83°) and CS/CJ-CDs (75.61°) were lower than that of control film (P < 0.05). Among all films, CS/J-CDs film had the lowest WCA (P < 0.05). Generally, the contact angle's magnitude is affected by the composition of the films (type and levels of biopolymer, plasticizers and antimicrobials, etc.) and their processing, which dictate the material's surface energy (Hernández, Ludueña and Flores, 2024). Generally, WCA value of film was reduced, owing to the hydrophilicity of CDs. CDs might interact with CS film components via hydrogen bond. Consequently, the polar groups were less available. CS/CJ-CDs film attained higher WCA value than J-CDs added film. Thus, CDs added films generally showed enhanced hydrophilicity of the resulting CS films.

Overall, the mechanical and water vapor barrier properties of CS/CJ-CDs film were better than other films. Better performance of the CJ-CDs than individual C-CDs and J-CDs likely resulted from the synergistic effects via the combination of the unique properties of each type of carbon dots. C-CDs and J-CDs had different surface functional groups due to their distinct precursor or raw materials and they exhibited varying bioactivities. As a result, the usage of mixed CDs from two different compounds improved overall performance, compared to the usage of one type of CDs alone.

L*, a*, b* and ΔE* of different films are presented in Table 2 . L* value (Lightness) of the control film was 91.04. In general, both polymers have more transparent in nature. Blending ratio of 1:1 had no effect on transparency of the CS film. The incorporation of yellowish CDs altered the appearance and transparency of resulting films. The addition of various CDs at same level (3 %) to the CS film resulted in different color values. Inclusion of C-CDs and J-CDs had reduced L* value (33.60 and 17.87 %) and increased a* (325.32 and 90.26 %) and b* values (400.58 and 290.50 %), when compared to those of control film. This result was in accordance with the previous reports (Khan et al., 2023a; Murugan et al., 2024; Riahi et al., 2024). Pronounced b* value was owing to the yellowish-brown CDs added into biopolymeric film. Color of the CS/J-CDs film was lighter and had less redness and yellowness than other CDs added films (Fig. 1). This was directly associated with the initial color of different CDs. ΔE* value of the resulting films also varied and related with L*, a* and b* values. Lowest ΔE* value was found in the control film (P < 0.05). The optical properties of films are often changed by adding active materials, especially those with pigments (Murugan et al., 2024). Thus, CDs had the direct impact on the color of the CS film, particularly in terms of yellowness.

Light transmission spectra of CS films are depicted in Fig. 2 a. The transmittance rate of CDs added film decreased in both UV and visible regions, when compared to control film. Peak was noticeable at 280 nm, demonstrating the unique π-π* transition of the C–O group in polymers. In general, the presence of amino groups in the chemical composition of chitosan rendered the UV blocking property (Ezati et al., 2023), whereas agar did not exhibit a band in the UV region. Inclusion of 3 % of CDs in CS film provided an excellent UV light barrier capability, which was reflected by the zero value observed in UV regions. Similar documentation was reported previously (Khan et al., 2023a; Murugan et al., 2024). Moreover, there is noticeable difference observed between CS/J-CDs and CS/C-CDs films. CS/C-CDs exhibited more effective blocking ability against UV light than CS/J-CDs film. Nonetheless, CS/CJ-CDs film had the improved UV barrier property than CS/J-CDs film, indicating the combined effect of CDs from both jik and cashew leaves.

Food spoilage is caused by UV-induced oxidation of food. Hence, it is crucial for packaging to have UV protection to avoid the direct contact with food ingredients. Chemical preservatives are commonly utilized to prevent food spoilage; however, they may result in long-term side effects in some individuals (Ezati et al., 2023). CS film could block both UV-A (69.49 %) and UV-B (89.98 %) (P < 0.05). Moreover, there was an enhancement noticed for CDs added films, especially for C-CDs added film (CS/C-CDs), which showed the highest UV protection among all the films. It blocked 98.16 % of UV-A and 99.88 % of UV-B (P < 0.05) (Fig. 2b). CS/J-CDs film exhibited the lowest UV-A and UV-B and there was an improved UV-barrier property exhibited for CS/CJ-CDs film. It clearly indicated that the CDs from natural sources had a greater ability to block UV lights, but the blocking efficiencies were mostly dependent on the sources of CDs. Improved UV barrier property was also observed for various natural CDs including green tea (Khan et al., 2023a) and guava leaf (Murugan et al., 2024).

Opaqueness is a crucial parameter in a consumer point of view. In composite film, the control film had lower opaqueness value (2.88), denoting its higher transparency. Incorporation of yellowish-brown CDs enhanced the opaqueness of CS film. Opaqueness value of resultant CDs added films was increased by 56.94, 21.18 and 59.38 % for CS/C-CDs, CS/J-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films, respectively (Table 2). The finding coincided with the former reports (Khan et al., 2023a; Murugan et al., 2024). Addition of J-CDs added film alone had lower reduction in transparency than C-CDs added films (P < 0.05) and there were no significant differences between control film and CS/J-CDs films (P > 0.05). As a result, the presence of J-CDs in CS film could maintain the transparency of CS film.

Functional groups and secondary structure of CS films are illustrated in Fig. 3 . The broad absorption bands at 3274, 3275, 3283 and 3286 cm-1 were observed for CS, CS/C-CDs, CS/J-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films, respectively, which were attributed to the vibrations of water (-OH). Distinctive peaks observed at 2912 and 2874 cm-1 (CS and CS/C-CDs), 2915 and 2874 cm-1 (CS/J-CDs) and 2914 and 2874 cm-1 (CS/CJ-CDs) corresponded to the stretching vibration of -C-H or -CH2 groups (Khan et al., 2023b). C=O stretching (amide I) vibrations were detected at the wavenumbers of 1556 (CS and CS/J-CDs), 1558 (CS/C-CDs) and 1557 cm-1 (CS/CJ-CDs), respectively. Peaks detected at 1638 and 1636 cm-1 for the control film and CDs added films were related to the bending vibration of N–H (amide II) (Riahi et al., 2024). O-H bending vibration of chitosan were recorded at 1406 (CS and CS/J-CDs), 1405 (CS/C-CDs) and 1407 cm-1 (CS/CJ-CDs) (Riahi et al., 2024). Characteristic peaks observed at 1150, 1076 and 1016 cm-1 for all films assigned to the C-O stretching vibrations of chitosan/starch composite film structure (Khan et al., 2023b; Wang et al., 2019). Changes in the intensity of several peaks were recorded after the inclusion of nano-sized filler (CDs). These changes could plausibly be due to the electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds between the components. Overall, FTIR spectra analysis demonstrated that the functional groups in the films interact with one other and the inclusion of CDs altered the displacement and intensity of some peaks. These results indicated that CDs might induce the alteration in functional groups and had a subtle effect on the molecular structure of the films.

Rate of CDs released from CS films into different simulant solutions (water and ethanol (10, 50 and 95 %)) is depicted in Fig. 4 . During the initial 30 min, the rate of CDs release from all films was higher in both water and a 10 % ethanol solution (P < 0.05). Nevertheless, the rate of release increased steadily after 30 min. There was only slight difference observed between the release of all CDs from CS films, plausibly due to the similar level of CDs (3 %). Moreover, when comparing the release of CDs from different CDs added films, the release of C-CDs was non-significantly higher than others (P > 0.05). The release rate was observed to be higher in water compared to ethanol due to its hydrophilic properties of all CDs. Nevertheless, the release rate in 50 and 95 % ethanol solutions was notably reduced up to 180 min, when compared to water and 10 % ethanol used as the media. In general, as ethanol concentrations increased, the rate of release decreased. The rate at which CDs are released from CS films depended on the polarity or hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity of the medium. The release rate from all films declined after 150 min, when water and 10 % ethanol were employed as media. The speed of release was influenced by the solubility of the film matrix and the affinity of CDs toward the solvents used. Because of the biopolymeric films' hydrophilic properties, water was able to easily penetrate through the film structure and effectively hydrate it. Consequently, a weak or looser structure could assist in the release of CDs from the film (Murugan et al., 2024). Conversely, ethanol solutions did not pass through CS polymers with ease. Due to this fact, CDs were confined to the matrix, limiting their movement and release into the media. Releasing CDs at a higher rate in a water-based medium might boost the bioactive effects of the film when it was in contact with food surfaces, where the moisture is the major constituent like fresh raw foods.

The process of food oxidation resulted in a series of reactions, generating free radicals that affect the food quality. Antioxidants have been incorporated into food packaging to prevent lipid oxidation (Zhang et al., 2022). Recently, both natural and metal based CDs were used as antioxidant agents in foods (Deepika et al., 2023). The control film and CDs added films had excellent DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activities (Table 3 ). Antioxidant activity of control (CS) film was owing to the hydroxyl, carboxyl and amide groups in the chitosan polymers (Khan et al., 2023b). The presence of -OH groups on the aromatic rings of polyphenols in natural sources gives them remarkable antioxidant properties, making them a valuable source of antioxidants with health benefits (Swallah, Sun, Affoh, Fu, & Yu, 2020). CS/C-CDs film had higher DPPH RS-A and ABTS RS-A than CS/J-CDs film. It indicated that the sources of CDs were responsible for its antioxidant activity. CS/C-CDs and CS/J-CDs films achieved higher scavenging activity of DPPH (18.17 and 15.24 µmol GA/10 g sample, respectively) and ABTS (68.37 and 45.28 µmol GA/10 g sample, respectively) (P < 0.05) than control film. CDs with oxygenated and amide functional groups underwent proton transfer and are able to eliminate free radicals (Roy et al., 2024). Moreover, CS/CJ-CDs film had the increased antioxidant activity in comparison with CS/J-CDs film. CDs from cashew leaves could help upsurge antioxidant activity of CDs from jik leaves. Notably, the RS-A of DPPH was lower than RS-A of ABTS, plausibly due to hydrophilic nature of CDs (Murugan et al., 2024). No FRA-P and MC-A were recorded for control (CS) film. Both FRA-P and MC-A also followed the same trend as of RS-As (DPPH and ABTS). CS/C-CDs achieved higher values of FRA-P and MC-A than CS/J-CDs. Antioxidant activity of polymeric film was dependent on the level of CDs incorporated (Khan et al., 2023a; Murugan et al., 2024). This finding reported that the same level (3 %) of CDs addition enhanced the antioxidant activity differently, which mostly depends on the size, sources and mixed action of CDs.

The antibacterial effectiveness of films containing CDs was evaluated using quantitative colony counts of E. coli and L. monocytogenes and the results are illustrated in Fig. 5 . For the positive control (without films), no antimicrobial activity against both microbes were obtained. Microbial growth was progressively increased up to 12 h for the positive control (P < 0.05). CS/J-CDs film was more effective than CS/C-CDs film in inhibiting E. coli. In contrast, CS/C-CDs film exhibited stronger activity than CS/J-CDs film against L. monocytogenes (P < 0.05). This clearly indicated that sources of CDs were responsible for the stronger activity against both Gram positive and negative strains. Antibacterial effects of CDs are governed by numerous factors such as surface functional groups, electrostatic interactions, small size, concentration and the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Travlou et al., 2018). CS/CJ-CDs showed strongest activity against both E. coli and L. monocytogenes, amongst all films. This could be due to the combined action of C-CDs against L. monocytogenes and J-CDs against E. coli. Moreover, CDs disrupt metabolic processes by binding to specific molecules in the microbe's cell wall and altering cell signals, leading to impaired function. When antimicrobial agents function, the bacterial cell wall needs to absorb antimicrobial substances, either by direct means or through electrostatic interaction. When the chemical penetrates through the cell wall, it causes internal components to leak out, destroys proteins, condenses chromosomes and interrupts DNA replication (Wang et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2016). Thus, the effect of size and source of CDs majorly influenced the antibacterial activity of resulting films.

Thermal properties determine the ability of the packaging films to withstand various external temperatures. Thermal degradation was analyzed over a temperature range of 30–800 °C, revealing weight loss in four distinct stages for the CS films (Fig. 6 and Table 4 ). The initial phase (Td1) of weight reduction (Δw1 = 8.74–9.74 %) occurred at temperatures of 49.37, 52.38, 57.97 and 56.94 °C for the CS, CS/C-CDs, CS/J-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films, respectively. The second stage of weight loss (Δw2 = 51.94–53.86 %) was recorded at Td2 of 261.30, 253.74, 252.44 and 252.44 °C for the CS, CS/C-CDs, CS/J-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films, respectively. A change in weight loss between 5.64–6.30 % (Δw3 ) was found at temperatures of 449.82, 443.45, 426.18 and 429.25 °C for the CS, CS/C-CDs, CS/J-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films, respectively. Final stage of weight loss (Δw4 = 1.91–2.34 %) was found at Td4 of 633.44, 634.87, 646.61 and 617.32 °C for the CS, CS/C-CDs, CS/J-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films, respectively. The initial weight loss (Δ1) was primarily due to the gradual decrease of water and other volatile elements present in the film matrix (Murugan et al., 2023). Weight loss observed in second phase (Δ2) was linked to the thermal degradation of chitosan and starch molecules. Additionally, the weight loss of Δ3 was more likely due to the result of the liberation of closely attached water, glycerol, short-chain, or low-molecular-weight molecules (Tagrida et al., 2023). Final weight loss (Δ4) might be owing to the breakdown of closely linked molecules with high molecular weight polymers in the film structure. CDs added film exhibited higher Td1 and Td4 than that of CS film. After heating, the remaining mass was found at the value of 29.81, 28.55, 29.21 and 31.34 % for the CS, CS/C-CDs, CS/J-CDs and CS/CJ-CDs films. Among all films tested, CS/CJ-CDs film contained higher residue, which indirectly represented the enhanced thermal stability. Therefore, the inclusion of CDs had a profound effect on the thermal stability of the CS film.

Multi-functional composite film from chitosan/starch containing various CDs (C-CDs, J-CDs and CJ-CDs) was successfully prepared and their properties were greatly improved. CS/C-CDs showed enhanced antioxidant activities amongst all other films. CS/CJ-CDs exhibited potent antimicrobial activity against L. monocytogenes and E. coli. CJ-CDs loaded film showed improved properties than films incorporated with C-CDs and J-CDs, which mostly due to the combined action of both C-CDs and J-CDs. Usage of mixed CDs from both cashew and jik leaf powders gained enhanced bioactivities. Thus, the addition of CDs from leaf powders to the biopolymeric films could act as a promising active packaging material for maintaining the quality and safety of food products. However, the efficacy could be varied, depending on the sources of CDs.

This research was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022R1A2B5B02001422) and Prince of Songkla University (PSU), Hat Yai under the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT) Research Chair grant (N42A670596).
